About This Project

My Connection to Peru

A personal, academic, and spiritual exploration of the land I call home.

My Ties to Peru

I was born and raised in Peru — it is my home country, the place that shaped who I am. Growing up, I experienced firsthand the breathtaking contrasts of Peruvian geography: the dry coastal city, the towering Andes visible on clear days, and the stories of jungle regions that felt like another world entirely.

My family is Peruvian, and though I now study at Ensign College in Salt Lake City, Utah, Peru remains deeply embedded in my identity. The food, the culture, the language, and the landscapes are all part of how I see the world.

Studying the earth science of Peru is not just an academic exercise for me — it is a way of understanding the physical forces that created the land where my family lives and where I grew up. Every earthquake, every river, every mountain range is personal.

One Fascinating Physical Geography Fact

Peru contains all 32 of the world's climate zones.

According to the World Meteorological Organization, there are 32 recognized climate types on Earth — and Peru is one of the only countries in the world that contains all of them within its borders. This extraordinary fact is possible because of the interaction between:

  • The Andes Mountains, which create dramatic altitude gradients (sea level to 6,768 m)
  • The Humboldt Current, a cold ocean current that creates coastal desert conditions
  • The Amazon Basin, which generates its own humid tropical climate system
  • Peru's tropical latitude, which intensifies solar radiation and moisture

No other country of similar size has this level of climatic variety. It is one reason Peru is considered one of the world's megadiverse nations — the variety of climates directly drives the variety of ecosystems and species.

The Earth as a Witness of God

"All things denote there is a God; yea, even the earth, and all things that are upon the face of it, yea, and its motion, yea, and also all the planets which move in their regular form do witness that there is a Supreme Creator."

— Alma 30:44, Book of Mormon

When I study Peru's earth processes, I see this scripture come alive. The physical world of Peru — its mountains, rivers, earthquakes, and ecosystems — bears a profound testimony of intelligent design and divine creation.

The Andes

The Andes are the longest continental mountain range on Earth — 7,000 km stretching across South America. Their formation through tectonic plate subduction follows precise physical laws. The order and scale of this process speaks of a Creator who engineers with intention, not randomness.

The Humboldt Current

This cold ocean current brings nutrient-rich water to Peru's coast, supporting one of the world's most productive marine ecosystems. The interconnection between ocean temperature, wind patterns, and marine life reveals a system too finely balanced to be accidental.

Amazon Biodiversity

Peru's Amazon region contains an estimated 50,000 plant species and thousands of unique animal species. The complexity and beauty of life that has adapted to every ecological niche speaks to the creative power of a God who delights in variety and life.

Tectonic Stability in Motion

Peru sits on the Nazca Plate — a region of constant tectonic activity. Yet Earth's crust, though in motion, follows consistent and predictable laws of physics. This regularity allows civilization to exist. The very order within the earth's motion testifies of divine governance.

To study earth science is to read one of God's oldest texts. Every stratum of rock, every river carving a canyon, every seed germinating in Andean soil is a page in that book. Peru — with its extraordinary diversity of natural processes — offers an unusually rich testimony that the earth was designed with purpose, beauty, and care.

Peru Earth Science · Ensign College · 2026
